nativeRange.habitat: Found along reef edges and upper slopes, typically in areas of brisk water movement, at depths of 1 to 15 metres.

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on invertebrates including small crustaceans, zooplankton, polychaete worms, and coral polyps. In captivity, generally accepts frozen meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, krill, chopped clam, mussel, and vitamin-enriched brineshrimp. Has been known to accept flake and pellet foods once settled. Feeding several small portions throughout the day tends to work better than single large meals, particularly for newly introduced specimens that can be reluctant to feed initially.

avoidWith: conspecifics unless introduced as a mated pair

imageCandidates.notes: Storm Martin, 1024x768. Crisp in-situ side profile of a live, vibrant adult clearly showing the two black triangular saddle patches, yellow dorsal, narrow dark lateral stripes on white body, and black eye band; whole fish framed. Best hero shot.

Bernard E. Picton, 1024x768. Sharp, naturally lit full side profile on dark reef background; all diagnostic markings (twin black saddles, dark lateral stripes, black eye band, yellow rear) clearly visible.

Frederic Ducarme, 2667x2000. High-res sharp in-situ shot among Acropora; whole fish framed with clear twin saddles and lateral stripes. Slight cyan/green underwater colour cast, so kept as supporting image.

summary: The Saddleback Butterfly is a bold, active butterflyfish from the Indian Ocean, recognised by its white body, yellow dorsal coloration, and twin black saddle patches. It is generally considered moderately challenging and is best suited to fish-only or FOWLR systems, as it tends to nip at corals and invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Saddleback Butterfly (Chaetodon falcula) is also sold under the names Falcula Butterflyfish and Sickle Butterflyfish. It is predominantly white with yellow dorsal coloration, narrow black lateral stripes, two black saddle patches on the back, and a black band through the eye — a distinctive pattern that makes it one of the easier butterflyfish to identify in the trade.

This species is generally considered not reef-safe. It has been reported to nip at hard and soft corals, sea anemones, mushroom anemones, gorgonians, sponges, and tubeworms. For this reason it is generally advised to keep it in a fish-only with live rock (FOWLR) system — a setup that provides natural structure and hiding places without the invertebrates and corals it would likely damage in a mixed reef.

The Saddleback Butterfly tends to be semi-aggressive toward conspecifics (members of its own species) and other butterflyfish with a similar appearance, but is generally more tolerant of dissimilar tankmates. In the wild it has been documented forming monogamous pairs, and a mated pair can often be maintained together in captivity. Single specimens may be somewhat territorial.

New specimens can be reluctant to accept prepared foods and may take time to settle into a feeding routine. Offering a variety of small meaty items — such as mysis shrimp, finely chopped crustacean flesh, and vitamin-enriched brineshrimp — several times daily is advisable until the fish is well established. Maintaining consistent, high water quality is reported to be important for long-term health.
